main_ingredients: tempura batter (wheat flour, egg, cold water)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of mushroom tempura consists of approximately 6–8 pieces weighing around 120 g, as commonly served as a side dish or appetiser in Japanese restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Mushroom Tempura — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record carries HPB provenance with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. Core macros and secondary nutrients are taken directly from the HPB record. Saturated fat and fatty acid fractions are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy reference for vegetable tempura as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this item.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; tempura batter / wheat-flour-battered fried food analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for mushroom tempura exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on wheat-flour tempura batter analogy; tempura batter is a refined wheat-flour product with a GI consistent with other lightly battered fried foods (GI 60–70 range). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (65) × available carbohydrate per serving (22.23 g × 1.2 serving weight / 100) / 100 = 65 × 26.68 / 100 = 17.3 for 120 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (71 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Mushroom tempura itself is low in sodium at 71 mg/100 g and is acceptable for hypertensive individuals, but tempura dipping sauce (tentsuyu) is very high in sodium and should be avoided or used in minimal quantities.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High fat (23.5 g/100 g) combined with medium-GI refined batter carbohydrates (22.2 g/100 g) impairs postprandial glycaemic control
type2_diabetes.guidance: Mushroom tempura is not recommended for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to the combination of high fat from deep-frying and medium-GI refined wheat batter, which together elevate postprandial blood glucose and impair insulin sensitivity; portion should be strictly limited and grilled or steamed mushrooms preferred.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (23.5 g/100 g) including saturated fat (~3.2 g) and estimated trans fat (~0.2 g/100 g) from deep-frying
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The deep-frying process introduces significant total fat, saturated fat, and trace trans fat, which are pro-atherogenic; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption and choose steamed or grilled mushroom preparations instead.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) and trans fat (~0.2 g/100 g), both strongly pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Mushroom tempura is unsuitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis because deep-frying generates AGEs and introduces trans fat, both of which promote systemic inflammation; steamed, grilled, or sautéed mushrooms are strongly preferred.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mushroom tempura (きのこの天ぷら, kinoko no tempura) is a Japanese dish consisting of assorted fresh mushrooms — commonly shiitake, shimeji, maitake, or enoki — coated in a light, cold-water wheat-flour-and-egg batter and deep-fried in vegetable oil until crisp and golden. The result is a delicate, lightly crunchy exterior encasing tender, umami-rich mushroom flesh. Tempura is a cornerstone of Japanese cuisine, introduced via Portuguese influence in the 16th century, and mushroom tempura is widely served as a side dish, appetiser, or component of a tempura set (天ぷら定食) in Japanese restaurants globally, including in Singapore. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 316 kcal, 3.99 g protein, 23.49 g fat, and 22.23 g carbohydrate, with 3.12 g dietary fibre and only 71 mg sodium. The high fat content arises from oil absorption during deep-frying. The refined wheat batter carries a medium glycaemic index (~65), making this dish a moderate glycaemic load food. Clinically, it is not recommended for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to the fat–carbohydrate combination impairing insulin sensitivity, and it is unsuitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients due to AGE generation and trace trans fat from frying. It is acceptable for hypertensive individuals when consumed without dipping sauce. The dish is vegetarian but not vegan due to egg in the batter, and contains gluten allergens.
common_user_queries: How many calories in mushroom tempura?
Is mushroom tempura high in sodium?
What mushrooms are used in tempura?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. Saturated fat and fatty acid fractions estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy vegetable tempura reference. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database framework for wheat-flour battered fried foods. All sources are approved under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
notes: Saturated fat (3.2 g/100 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (10.1 g/100 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (8.4 g/100 g), and cholesterol_mg (18 mg/100 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for vegetable tempura (closest approved-source analogue) as HPB does not separately report fatty acid fractions for this item. Trans_fat_g (0.2 g/100 g) is an estimate based on typical deep-frying oil trans fat generation documented in peer-reviewed literature; this value is not directly reported by HPB. Potassium_mg (220 mg/100 g) is an estimate from USDA SR Legacy mushroom tempura analogue. calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for mushroom tempura as prepared are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for this specific composite dish as of 2026. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for mushroom tempura exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 120 g serving = 65 × (22.23 × 1.2) / 100 = 65 × 26.68 / 100 = 17.3.
